sentences.text: Can I see that name again?
Yeah.
From this side it sounded-- it looked so different here from this side.
[LAUGHS] That's why I wondered.
Yeah.
My name is Izchak, I-Z-C-H-A-K, Goldblatt, my last name, G-O-L-D-B-L-A-T-T.
Yeah.
I was known by the name of Icu.
That would be I-C-U, Icu.
That's how my friends called me.
That's how they called me in the house.
Birth date is May 5, 1927.
Born in Sighet, Romania.
S-I-G-H-E-T.
Romania is R-U-M-A-N-I-A.
That's where I grew up till I was 17.
Childhood-- well, I didn't really have a childhood.
I always liked to say that I was born old, and I'm getting younger every day.
And I'm trying to prove that by playing tennis and ping pong as much as can-- as I can.
But there was a childhood.
I was a child.
I remember when I was a kid.
We lived in a village near Sighet at the beginning, and-- where my father was the notary of three villages.
It was Upper [?
Rona, ?] Lower [?
Rona ?] and [?
Ronasak. ?] Those-- those were the names of the three villages.
Well, as it-- why did I say where I wasn't a child?
I didn't have a childhood because there was no time to play.
You got up at 6 o'clock in the morning.
I went with my two brothers, two of my brothers.
I went to cheder till-- from 6:00 to 8:00. 8 o'clock we went to school, to public school.
That was 'till 12.
And then we head back to cheder and we had a sandwich-- kind of a sandwich, bread and butter for lunch-- and we continued there till 2 o'clock. 2 o'clock we went back to school.
It was till 4 o'clock.
After 4:00, we went back to cheder until about 8:30, 9 o'clock.
It was a long day, no time to play, no recreation time.
I mean, we had-- about 5 o'clock, we had a half an hour to play a little in the courtyard and eat something if we had what to eat, which wasn't the always the case.
But-- that's all.
It was a long, hard day, and when you came home, you were already very, very tired.
So I don't remember ever having a toy or-- or a ball to play with.
I mean, if we did play with a ball, which I liked-- I liked soccer-- we played with a-- we took two-- two socks, and we huddled them together and made a ball out of it.
That's how we played ball.
So that was till about 10 years.
When I was 10 years old, we went into the city.
The Hungarians came in, and immediately my father-- the next day my father was fired from his job.
So there was no-- nothing to live on.
So we moved into Sighet, which was about 20 kilometers from-- from the village we lived in.
And there again, cheder, school, cheder, school, cheder, school.
And then it was Hungary, not Romania?
Then it was Hungary.
Then it was already Hungary.
I-- I remember an episode where the same teacher who-- because this-- this place, Sighet, was-- when I was born it was Romania, then it was Hungaria, then it was Hungary again, and Romania again.
So the same teacher taught us in the Hungarian school, the same teacher that me in fifth class in the-- in the-- in the Romanian school.
So I was one of the best students also-- always in the class.
So he said something, and I say to him the answer.
He said, no.
It cannot be.
It cannot be that you don't know.
But I didn't know the Hungarian language.
I only knew a little of it.
So he called me behind the-- the blackboard and said to me, tell it to me in [?
Romanian. ?] And I said it.
He said, yeah, that's right, but you don't say that that way in Hungarian.
It was a funny little episode.
And whenever we got a little time in the-- in the-- in the public school-- because in Jewish life there was no time that you would spend without learning Torah.
In the public school, you got that half hour and again a half hour, two half four hours off.
Then we played soccer in the-- in the courtyard with all the boys.
And I was very fast, always been fast running and we had a good time.
We were nine children, five boys and four girls.
One of my brothers I don't remember because he died when I was a baby.
He was 13 years old.
He went to his Shiva.
When he came home, he got the cold.
He got pneumonia, and there were no-- there was no penicillin yet.
So he died of that malady.
In fact, I remember, I was myself down with a cold, and I was very, very sick of a very high temperature.
And I can remember, I can see my mother standing and praying, oh god-- oh god, don't take this one too.
I was so-- they didn't have what to give you.
So she bathed me in cold towels.
And I remember that she was so excited that she dropped the thermometer and the whole quicksilver, the quicksilver that was the arsenic, was spreading out on the floor.
And I remember, at that moment I-- I started feeling better.
When I heard the-- the knock of the-- of the glass breaking, I started feeling better and better and better.
What else do I tell you about my--
--brothers and sisters?
Sighet had a population of 30,000 inhabitants, out of which 15,000 or so were Jewish.
And, of course, all except one or two businesses were Jewish.
And on Sabbath, you walked around.
There was nothing-- nothing open.
It was just like a holiday that everything was closed, because there were only like two shops that were not-- in non-Jewish-- by non-Jewish people.
And there was one shop, a Jewish shop, that was open on Sabbath, and-- and on holidays, on Jewish holidays.
Other than that, everything was closed.
Everybody went to shul in the morning, shul in the afternoon.
In the meantime, we played around.
Us kids, we played a little soccer, which we were not supposed to do, but we did it anyway.

Well, very, very strictly religious-- we were so religious that-- that after the war, when we were liberated and we were eating whatever we were-- we-- we could find-- I mean, later I will tell you an episode when we were digging.
It was about 10 days before the ending of the-- before the Americans came to free us.
We were digging a-- something, a ditch.
And there was a bone.
And we were like 10 people.
All of us suddenly jumped on that bone.
Who could grab it?
And the happy one who grabbed it washed the bone, and ate it until there was-- it was possible to eat.
And when it was too hard-- the part that was so hard that you couldn't eat-- we burned it, and he continued to-- to eat it.
So you didn't know it was a bone of a human being, or an animal, or how old it was.
You didn't care.
You were so hungry that you would-- you would just eat everything.
But we were so religious that even after the war, when we ate those conserves, those cans of meat, and it was not kosher-- it was whatever it was.
We didn't even know what it was.
We didn't care what it was, but I would make a blessing.
In my-- in my mind, I would make a blessing, because we make a blessing before eating something and after eating something, because the Talmud has it that everything belongs to God.
And if you take something without-- without saying a blessing, then you are kind of stealing from God.
And you can't do that.
So you say the blessing.
By the blessing, you acquire it, and it belongs to you, and then you can eat it.
So even after eating non-kosher food, I would make the blessing, or when I drank a little water, I would make that certain blessing before-- before drinking it.
I remember, we were very close to Elie Wiesel, who is now a very famous person, maybe the most famous Jew-- non-religious Jew-- When did you first meet him?
Oh, we were together in school in Sighet.
We were-- we were like close friends, very-- like the two more-- shall we say the best-- better scholars.
Were very good at the Talmud, studying Talmud-- but we were so religious that on Shabbat we wouldn't speak at all, because Hebrew we didn't know.
We only knew a few words of [INAUDIBLE], of the ancient Hebrew language, not enough to speak freely.
And we didn't want to speak Yiddish as not to [SPEAKING HEBREW] through a foreign language.
So we-- we-- we didn't speak.
We just didn't speak.
That's how religious we were.
From 8 o'clock in the morning, if-- if there wasn't school, from 8 o'clock in the morning to-- to 8 o'clock in the evening or to 7 o'clock in the evening.
We-- we were studying from the same [INAUDIBLE].
We were not just friends in the same cheder.
We were studying in the same [INAUDIBLE], and then we were a little bit older, like 14, we were studying six boys together in-- in-- at one of our friends' parents' sukkah.
They had a built sukkah where we had-- we had one teacher who was teaching only the six of us.

My parents couldn't afford paying my-- for my tuition, but the-- the other parents, every parent wanted his boy to go to gather with me in cheder to study together with me because I was a very quiet boy and a very learned boy.
At six, I would cite a page of Talmud every Shabbos by heart.

Although I started with two of my brothers together in the same cheder, and my brothers are-- one is three years and one is four years older than me-- but I was the one-- I was the kind of one who could remember a whole page of-- of Talmud by heart for every Shabbos and I used to get a piece of chocolate, which was a big thing for us.
We didn't see so much chocolate in the house.
We were happy to see bread.
It's very difficult to talk about happy memories.
I-- there-- there were not-- happy memories I really don't have.
I mean, there were happy moments if I kicked a goal at soccer on the soccer game.
I was very good, but happy memories per se as a child-- it is even difficult for me to-- to imagine myself as a child.
I was never a child.
I was so-- so serious.
I can remember I started going to cheder when I was three years old.
So they taught me to pray, to read the alphabet and to pray.
Then I knew that, and I knew it very fast because I had a photographic memory.
I-- they gave me to learn the Torah, [INAUDIBLE].
Immediately I knew that.
They started with [INAUDIBLE].
Later, they started with [INAUDIBLE]. and I tell you by six-- I see boys today in America, by six they don't know what a [INAUDIBLE] is yet.
They don't even know what it is.
And I already knew by heart pages, whole pages of [INAUDIBLE].
So there wasn't time for-- for happy moments.
This is very difficult.
Yeah, happy moments, yes, maybe-- maybe, yes-- when I used to go Shabbos afternoon, we used to go to the [INAUDIBLE] of the rabbi.
He was a, you know, a [INAUDIBLE] rabbi.
When we would sit at his table and hear him say Torah, or hear him recite this [INAUDIBLE], that-- those were my happy moments.
Those were our happy moments that I can recall-- nothing.
Personally, a happy moment at home-- the misery was so great that you can hardly imagine.
We lived 10 people in two rooms and a kitchen.
One was the lady-- the girls' rooms-- room, and the other was the-- the father and mother's.
My two elder brothers would-- would-- would sleep in the kitchen.
There was this kind of table who was also a bed.
In the evening, it was a bed.
In-- during the day it was covered and it was a table-- used as a table in the kitchen.
The bathroom was outside.
If you had to go in the evening to the bathroom, you would just hold back because it was dark.
There was no electricity till the last year.
We didn't have electricity.
I remember when I studied English-- and I studied English, by the way in Sighet-- and I used to-- to write, because when I wrote something down I remembered it forever.
I can-- I can quote to you something that I did in Cyprus in 1946 in English that I read.
But because I wrote it down, I remember it, and I can quote it, which is Faust, a certain thing-- difficult thing.
So water wasn't in the house.
Water was outside.
There was a well.
The water was very good, very sweet, very good water.
You never get water like that here in the States or-- or-- or in Europe or anyplace except if you live in a village or in a small place like those.
The water was very good, but you have to go out and bring it in from the well.
We had two cows to maintain us with milk.
And my sisters and my mother they would make butter, and they would make cheese out of that milk.
So only the-- the minimal-- the minimum was there.
My mother used to bake bread twice a week.
On Tuesday morning, she would get up at 3 o'clock in the morning, make the dough, bake the bread.
When the-- when the daughters were bigger they were-- they would help, especially one of them who lives in Israel now, because she wasn't working out of the house.
The others were working out of the house.
And that bread, the taste and the smell of that bread is something that you guys in America, who were born in America, will never know and never feel.
That that very good.
That was very good.
So you can say that Tuesday morning and Friday morning when she would make those pitas, special pitas for breakfast, that was a happy moment for me.
But those were the kind of moments that we-- that we had, happy moments that I have in recollection that I can recollect as a child, from my childhood.
Well, we knew about anti-semitism because they kicked my father out the minute the Hungarians took over.
The next day, he was out of his position.
But experiencing it per se-- the first instant that I remember anti-semitism was I had a-- a non-Jewish friend who every day came to my house after school that I make his homework for him.
And he was the first one to throw me off the sidewalk and say, [SPEAKING HUNGARIAN].
Jew-- in Hungarian-- Jew, down from the sidewalk, that I should walk in the middle of the road where the horses and the carriages went, not-- not the people.
He was the first one that-- that hurts me to this day, because we were such close friends.
I was every day at their house.
He was every day at my house.
We were neighbors.
We were playing soccer together.
I was making every day his lessons, because he was a nice, good looking boy, taller than me.
I was frail and thin, but I was the one with the knowledge to make the-- his lessons and mine as well, so-- and he was the one to give me the taste of anti-semitism for the first time.
Well, we saw the Germans-- we saw some Germans coming in and taking over the post office.
I happened to be at the post office to send a letter when two Germans came in.

One, they came with a jeep, with a kind of a-- kind of jeep type of car-- not the jeep, the German type of jeep-- and one of them was walking on the sidewalk in front of the post office, and the other one was a sergeant or a sergeant major, something like that, the feldwebel.
And he walked in and he took over the post office.
So then we saw Germans.
And the--
I was 17. 17.
Must have been very close.
I was born on May 5, and we were deported on May 9th.
So that must have been very close to that time.
And the anti-semitism in seeing in-- in the works, we have seen-- we have seen when they took us to the ghetto, because there were only Hungarian Gendarmes.
And the yelling and screaming is exactly like you saw on the if you saw the and they come and hit with the stick everything that is making noise to make-- to make-- to put the fear of God into you.
And that's exactly how it was.
They drove us out of the house, and they didn't give us too much time.
Everything was rush, rush, rush.
Well, we were aware, but we didn't believe it, because we went out to the train station, and we saw people being transported.
I don't know where they were going through.
The Czechoslovaks were running away, and they came through Sighet, through the train station of Sighet.
I don't know where that train took them.
I haven't got an idea, but we saw them.
We saw people evacuating.
We saw people evacuated and running from the Germans, and we-- we knew by the newspapers and by radio.
We new the-- the news, what's going on in Poland, what's going on in the world, in France, when they-- when they got into France.
We knew everything.
The-- the news reached us like they reached everybody else in the world.
But physically seeing them-- we saw Italian troops passing through Sighet when the Italians were still working together with the-- with the Germans, when they were still fighting.
Later I met Italians when they were prisoners.
They took their weaponry away, and they made them prisoners.
And we built houses.

And we built-- I was working with a firm that built their heating conditions, you know, their eating-- heating facilities so that the houses be heated, because it was very cold-- but they were prisoners of war.
They had more rights than we had.
They didn't work as hard.
In fact, I don't know if they worked at all.
They were-- they were confined to their-- to their houses where they were.
So at first, we saw the Italians walking through Sighet, and Germans we saw very little of, very few, very few.
Here and there you would see a German, but not really, not the army, not the German army we didn't see.
We knew what they are doing.
We knew what they are doing to-- to Poland.
We knew that the Czechs ran away from them.
We-- we have seen Jewish people who ran away who came-- very few, about two or three of them-- who came to our synagogues and told us about the atrocities of the Germans.
They were telling that they are simply digging huge pits and-- and-- and having the Jews take their clothes off.
The Jews had to dig their own graves, their own pits, and they would just shoot them in the line, like we know already later.
We saw them on films.
But at that time, they were telling us, and we said, no, they are crazy, because those people acted a little nervous.
And naturally, now we understand why, because if you go through something like that, you-- you cannot stay normal.
None of us survivors is 100% normal.
We have our fears to live with.
We have a lot of things to-- to remember, and-- and to relive.
Each time we talk about it, we have dreams at night.
I'm probably going to dream about this tonight also, because I dreamt when I spoke in front of those children that I mentioned before to you, in private, off camera.
I spoke-- I spoke in front of a high school.
There were like 120 boys from 15 to 18.
And I told them a very little bit about what I went through.
And they were so impressed.
And I-- I relived that moment, and every moment, and I thought I'm going to faint.
And that night I sure enough had my dreams, my bad dreams.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So really of the Germans we saw very little.
To the ghetto, they took us on the 9th of May, which is the week after Pesach, I mean the Sunday-- the first Sunday after Passover--
Yes.
Well, what they did was just sharpening their knives, as they say.
I mean the real doing was, you see that house where you live?
It's going to be mine.
I mean, they would walk by houses and point to the houses and the people who were still there and say, see that house?
I like that house.
This is-- this is what I'm going to take.
And the other one would say, no, I will take the other house, I will take-- so they were just talking.
Most of the time you would hear about beatings, up here and there.
They were cutting beards on the street.
If they find-- found somebody who had ear locks, they would cut the ear locks.
They would cut the beards, or they would just shear them off.
They would cut you on the street.
They would do that, but personally, I didn't have a beard, and my ear locks I had cut off before because at school they forced me to cut them off.
The Hungarians already forced you to cut off the--
--ear locks in the school.
They wouldn't allow you to.
We would go with the ear locks for a certain time behind our ears, but then the boys, you know, they would take them out, and the teacher, and they would say cut them off.
So we had to cut them off.
Yeah.
I went to study a trade, jewelry.
I had two brothers there in Budapest, my two brothers.
They went up to Budapest to make a living, because at home, we didn't have what to live on.
So they went, and they would make some money, and send home.
Each week or each month they would send a little bit of money home to my parents.
And I also went to learn to be a jeweler.
My-- all my brothers were jewelers.
I went to Budapest in 1941 or '42-- '41, And I came back in 1942.
I was there for a year.
Well, Budapest, per se, it was still nice.
At that time, it was still nice.
You just heard that the war-- the war was in Poland.
The war was far away, sort of.
Communication wasn't as good as it was now.
We knew about things, but we didn't know as much as-- as you would know now the way you can see everything on television.
In 1942, the British-- the British say that they would bomb Budapest, and then when my-- then my parents say that the youngest one, they wanted me home.
I am the child.
So they didn't want me to go through bombardments.
So Sighet was relatively a small city.
Who-- why-- why would they bomb Sighet?
Sighet had no importance, no military importance whatsoever.
So they called me home.
They thought I would be safe.
The two older brothers remained in Budapest and they went through the war.
One of them went through the war in Budapest.
He went into the Budapest ghetto and he was saved by his boss, by our boss.
We worked together, the two of us.
My other brother worked with somebody else.
He had a Jewish boss who was also deported and never came home.
And this Jakob Laszlo.
He was a non-Jew, he was an officer in the Hungarian army, and he brought some soldiers' clothes.
And gave them-- he dressed my brother and he took him out as a Hungarian soldier.
And then he put him in a Swedish house, one of those Wallenberg-- you know who Wallenberg was, Raoul Wallenberg.
And this is how he got through the war without being deported or without having to go work for the Germans.
My other brother, the one who is a little bit younger-- he's-- I mean, not younger than me, he's three years older than me-- He was in Yugoslavia in a working unit.
They were working for the-- they were digging the trenches for the soldiers, for the Hungarian soldiers.
They never-- they never gave them weapons.
And they were 2,000 people in a certain place called Bor in Yugoslavia.
And it's famous.
The Germans put fire to put the torch through that-- through their barracks, and only 19 escaped, and my brother was one of them, one of these 19.
